hook: You already know how to solve second-order differential equations like $ay'' + by' + cy = f(x)$ — now imagine the same ideas extended to equations with third, fourth, or even higher derivatives. Many real-world systems, from beam deflection in engineering to high-order electrical circuits, demand exactly this generalization. Can the techniques you've mastered scale up seamlessly, and what new tools will you need along the way?
concept: Every topic in this unit builds on one central idea: a higher-order linear differential equation has a solution space with a rich structure, and your job is to systematically find a basis for that space and then handle any forcing function thrown at you — formally captured by the theory of $n$th-order linear operators and their solution spaces.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to use the Wronskian determinant to rigorously verify whether a set of functions forms a linearly independent solution set for a higher-order equation.
Master solving homogeneous linear equations of order $n$ by finding the characteristic roots — real, repeated, or complex — and assembling the general solution from $n$ independent pieces.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Undetermined Coefficients for Higher Order Equations
topic_roadmap.description: Apply the method of undetermined coefficients to find particular solutions of higher-order equations driven by exponentials, polynomials, sines, and cosines.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Variation of Parameters for Higher Order Equations
topic_roadmap.description: Extend variation of parameters to $n$th-order equations, giving you a powerful method to find particular solutions even when the forcing function falls outside the reach of undetermined coefficients.

Solve Cauchy-Euler equations — where coefficients are powers of $x$ matching the order of each derivative — by using the substitution $x = e^t$ to transform them into constant-coefficient equations you already know how to handle.
